metadata.summary: Make debt visible using Fowler's quadrant. Track in TECH_DEBT_REGISTER.md with type, severity, location, evidence, and interest cost. Cap register at 20–30 items; agents scan, humans approve.

content.title: Technical Debt Basics
content.summary: A framework for making technical debt visible, typed, and actionable. Debt items are classified in Martin Fowler's quadrant (deliberate/inadvertent × reckless/prudent) and tracked in a TECH_DEBT_REGISTER.md with type, severity, location, evidence, and interest cost. Agents scan for candidates; humans approve before items are registered. Cap the register at 20–30 items.

why.p: Invisible debt accumulates silently into deployment friction, production incidents, and velocity loss. Fowler's quadrant prevents the false binary of "all debt is bad" — prudent-deliberate debt is a legitimate trade-off when logged and paid. Tracking interest cost (hours/week lost) makes debt tangible to stakeholders and enables evidence-based prioritization.

list.item: Sprint/quarterly planning: surface debt before picking payoff items.
Post-incident review: register the debt that caused the incident with severity and evidence.
New-codebase onboarding: inventory existing debt before estimating work.
Feature trade-off: deliberately taking prudent debt and logging it in the same commit.
Greenfield prototypes likely to be thrown away — registering debt is overhead with no reader.
Sub-100-line scripts where the debt framework is heavier than the code.
Code under active full rewrite — log the rewrite, not item-level debt deleted next week.
Teams with no payoff process — registering debt no one will pay is theatre.

section.name: fowler-quadrant
section.p: The foundational classification: two axes, four quadrants.

rule.statement: Classify every debt item on two axes: deliberate vs inadvertent, and reckless vs prudent — only then decide whether to register it.
rule.rationale: Not all debt is equal; prudent-deliberate debt is a legitimate trade-off; reckless-anything is a red flag for code review.

list.item: Deliberate + Reckless: "We don't have time for design" — HIGH RISK; block in code review.
Deliberate + Prudent: "We must ship now and deal with consequences" — STRATEGIC; log and schedule payoff.
Inadvertent + Reckless: "What's layering?" — INCOMPETENCE; fix with training and review.
Inadvertent + Prudent: "Now we know how we should have done it" — LEARNING; log and pay when convenient.

section.name: six-debt-types
section.p: Debt appears in six categories. Understand the type to know the payoff strategy.

list.item: Code Debt: messy code, duplication, poor naming.
Design Debt: poor architecture, tight coupling, violated SRP.
Test Debt: missing tests, flaky tests, tests without assertions.
Documentation Debt: outdated or missing docs, stale ADRs.
Infrastructure Debt: outdated dependencies, EOL runtimes, legacy deployment scripts.
Process Debt: missing CI/CD, manual deployments, no code review.

antipattern.description: Conflating code smells with debt: every TODO or long function gets flagged as high-severity debt.
antipattern.reason: Without a business-impact filter the register becomes noise; require concrete evidence (incident IDs, churn data, slow-query logs).

section.name: interest-cost-framing
section.p: The key insight: debt is measured not by severity but by interest — the cost of carrying it.

rule.statement: Each registered debt item must include an interest estimate: hours/week or hours/month of extra work caused by its presence.
rule.rationale: Interest cost converts abstract code quality into concrete business cost; enables stakeholder prioritization without technical jargon.

rule.statement: Require evidence for the interest estimate: incident IDs, PR latency data, slow-query traces, or churn statistics — no invented numbers.
rule.rationale: Agents default to high severity with confident numbers; without evidence the estimates are noise that erodes trust.

section.name: register-format
section.p: How to structure the debt register in markdown.

rule.statement: Cap the register at 20–30 items; anything below that is below the noise floor and will never be prioritized.
rule.rationale: Infinite registers demoralize teams; a capped list forces prioritization and keeps each item visible.

section.reference: Register template with all required fields.

section.name: measurement-metrics
section.p: Three dimensions track the health of debt management.

list.item: Code: cyclomatic complexity < 10 per function, duplication < 5%, coverage > 80%.
Velocity: feature implementation time, bug fix time, deployment frequency — trending direction matters more than absolute value.
Operational: production incidents/month, MTTR, deployment failure rate, rollback frequency.

rule.statement: Track payoff velocity (debt items closed per sprint), not absolute count — the count grows and demoralises teams.
rule.rationale: A growing register count does not mean the team is failing; closed-per-sprint measures actual progress.

section.name: agent-workflow
section.p: How to run debt scanning and registration with agents.

Run debt collection as a periodic batch job. Scanner subagent walks repo + git history + linter output and emits candidates with evidence. Triager subagent (Opus) deduplicates against existing register and classifies by Fowler quadrant. Human approves before items are appended to TECH_DEBT_REGISTER.md.

rule.statement: Never let an autonomous agent close a debt item; closure requires human verification of test improvement and metric improvement.
rule.rationale: Debt payoff rewrite risk is high; the human review on the pre-merge gate is non-negotiable.

section.reference: Script to emit candidate debt items as JSONL for triage.

section.name: agent-gotchas
section.p: Common failure modes when using agents to discover and track debt.

antipattern.description: Severity inflation: LLMs default every item to "high".
antipattern.reason: Require concrete impact metrics or automatically downgrade to "medium"; every "high" must cite an incident ID or quantified cost.

antipattern.description: Phantom debt: agent invents items in code it never read.
antipattern.reason: Force the agent to cite file:line and verify file existence; reject items without a resolvable path.

antipattern.description: Mixing TODO with debt: TODO != debt.
antipattern.reason: Filter on "has business impact" and "would not exist if redone today"; TODOs are implementation notes, not cost items.

antipattern.description: Stale references: debt items pointing at moved or renamed files.
antipattern.reason: Run a weekly link-check pass to verify all file:line references are still resolvable.

antipattern.description: Recursive debt: "Refactor the debt tracker itself" loops.
antipattern.reason: Cap recursion depth in the loop.

antipattern.description: Human checkpoint: letting an autonomous agent merge a debt-payoff PR.
antipattern.reason: The rewrite risk is high; require human review on the pre-merge gate.

section.name: subagent-workflow
section.p: Recommended agents for debt scanning and management.

list.item: Scanner subagent (Sonnet): runs radon, lizard, jscpd, git log and emits candidates as JSON.
Triager subagent (Opus): applies Fowler quadrant (deliberate/inadvertent × reckless/prudent), severity, business-impact estimate.
faion-sdd-executor-agent: turns approved debt items into SDD tasks with quality gates.
faion-improver skill: fits the "investigate → brainstorm → apply" loop for paying down a single debt item.

section.name: cli-tools-for-debt-scanning
section.p: Recommended tools to automate debt candidate discovery.

list.item: radon: Python complexity / maintainability index.
lizard: Multi-language complexity, oversize functions.
jscpd: Copy-paste detector across languages.
pip-audit: Python CVEs in dependencies (security debt).
npm audit / pnpm audit: JavaScript dependency CVEs.
dependabot / renovate: Outdated dependency detection (infra debt).
git-of-theseus: Code churn / age cohort plots.
tokei: Quick lines-of-code inventory.

section.name: services-and-platforms
section.p: Commercial and open-source platforms for debt tracking and prioritization.

list.item: SonarQube / SonarCloud: Technical Debt metric in days, per-file hotspots via REST API.
CodeScene: Behavioural code analysis, hotspot prioritisation by churn.
CodeClimate Quality: Maintainability scores, debt remediation cost.
Stepsize: Built specifically as a debt tracker tied to Jira/Linear.
DebtMap (OSS): Lightweight markdown debt register tooling.
Linear / Jira: Use a tech-debt label + saved view; agent files items via API.

section.name: prompt-patterns-for-agents
section.p: Example prompt structures to guide agent debt scanning and triage.
section.prompt: Scan <repo> for technical debt candidates. For each, output JSON: { "id", "type": "code|design|test|doc|infra|process", "severity": "low|med|high", "location": "<file:line>", "evidence": "<commit / linter rule / metric>", "estimated_payoff_tokens": <int> } Skip anything without concrete evidence. Max 30 items.

Triage candidates against existing register at TECH_DEBT_REGISTER.md. For each: dedupe (same root cause), classify in Fowler quadrant, estimate business impact (cite incidents/PR latency/etc.). Output diff of new register entries only.

template.purpose: Register template with ID, type, severity, location, evidence, interest, estimated payoff.
Emit candidate debt items as JSONL (complexity via lizard, missing tests, outdated deps).
